0:0.rendering: Now the word of Yahweh came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying,
0:0.note: This opening echoes the exact formula used for Elijah and other prophets, but Jonah's name means "dove," hinting at the flight to come.

0:1.rendering: Get up, go to the great city of Nineveh, and call out against it, because their evil has come up before my face.
0:1.note: The Hebrew word for "evil" here echoes the very first pages of Genesis, where human wickedness also "came up" before God and brought judgment.

0:2.rendering: But Jonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the presence of the LORD. He went down to Joppa, found a ship heading for Tarshish, paid its fare, and went down into it to sail with them away from the presence of the LORD.
0:2.note: The Hebrew repeats "from the presence of the LORD" like a drumbeat, showing Jonah's desperate, downward flight from God's call.

0:3.rendering: But Yahweh hurled a great wind upon the sea, and a mighty storm arose on the sea so that the ship threatened to break apart.
0:3.note: The Hebrew word for "hurled" is the same verb used later when the sailors hurl Jonah overboard, quietly framing the storm as God's first throw in this tale.

0:4.rendering: The sailors became afraid and each cried out to his own god. They hurled the cargo that was in the ship into the sea to lighten it for them. But Jonah had gone down into the innermost part of the vessel, lain down, and fallen sound asleep.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "innermost part" (yarkete) is the same one used for the "recesses" of Sheol, hinting that Jonah is already descending into a living death.

0:5.rendering: So the ship's captain came to him and said, "What are you doing sound asleep? Get up! Call on your god! Perhaps that god will think about us so that we do not perish."
0:5.note: The captain's urgent words echo the very language God used to commission Jonah at the book's beginning: "Get up!" and "Call on."

0:6.rendering: They said to one another, "Come, let us cast lots so we may know on whose account this evil has come upon us." So they cast lots, and the lot fell on Jonah.
0:6.note: In the ancient world, casting lots was a standard way to discern the gods' will in a crisis, not gambling, revealing the sailors' instinctive sense that someone had offended a deity.

0:7.rendering: Then they said to him, "Tell us now, because of whom has this disaster come on us? What is your occupation? Where do you come from? What is your country? And from what people are you?"
0:7.note: The sailors' rapid-fire questions echo ancient Near Eastern interrogation formulas used when disaster strikes, probing for the divine culprit behind the storm.

0:8.rendering: He said to them, I am a Hebrew, and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.
0:8.note: Jonah's confession echoes the ancient Near Eastern title for the Creator while revealing his ironic flight from the very God he claims to revere.

0:9.rendering: The men were filled with dread, and they said to him, "What is this you have done?" For the men knew that he was fleeing from the presence of the Lord, because he had told them.
0:9.note: The sailors' dread echoes the same Hebrew word for "fear" that will later describe their reverent worship in verse 16, showing how Jonah's disobedience first terrifies then transforms them.

0:10.rendering: Then they said to him, "What should we do to you so that the sea will calm down for us?" For the sea was growing more and more stormy.
0:10.note: The sailors' desperate question echoes the very words God used to call Jonah in verse 2, flipping "What evil has come up before me?" into "What shall we do to you?"

0:11.rendering: He said to them, Pick me up and hurl me into the sea. Then the sea will calm down for you, because I know it is on my account that this great storm has come upon you.
0:11.note: Jonah's words ironically echo the sailors' earlier plea to their gods, revealing his dawning awareness that Yahweh, not fate, controls the storm.

0:12.rendering: Nevertheless the men rowed hard to get back to dry land, but they could not, for the sea kept growing more stormy against them.
0:12.note: The sailors' desperate rowing echoes the earlier verb for "hurled" in verse 4, showing how the storm itself fights against their efforts to resist God's will.

0:13.rendering: Then they cried out to the LORD and said, "Ah, LORD, please do not let us perish for this man's life. Do not hold against us innocent blood, for you, LORD, have done as you pleased."
0:13.note: The sailors' prayer echoes the very words of the captain who urged Jonah to cry out to his God, now turned back toward the true LORD in desperate humility.

0:14.rendering: Then they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea. The sea stopped its raging.
0:14.note: The sailors' desperate act echoes the ancient Near Eastern motif of offering a life to calm chaotic waters, yet here it reveals God's sovereign control over the very storm he summoned.

0:15.rendering: The men feared the LORD with a great fear. They offered a sacrifice to the LORD and made vows.
0:15.note: The pagan sailors' awe and worship echo the very fear Israel was called to show its God.

0:16.rendering: Now the Lord appointed a great fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.
0:16.note: The Hebrew word for "appointed" echoes God's sovereign control over creation, from sea monsters to tiny worms.
